content:
# Deuteronomy 25 — Disagreement Log
**SOPHIA** | EMET vs. LOGOS vs. PNEUMA | 19 verses

---

## V.1 — Opening formula

- **EMET:** "When there is a dispute between men, and they draw near to the judgment, and the judges judge them — they shall declare the righteous one righteous and condemn the wicked one."
- **LOGOS:** "When men have a dispute and come to court, and the judges decide between them, they shall acquit the innocent and condemn the guilty."
- **PNEUMA:** "When people have a dispute and bring it to court, the judges will hear the case and decide — acquitting the innocent and condemning the guilty."

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "the righteous one righteous" (hitsdiqu et-ha-tsaddiq); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "acquit the innocent." The Hebrew tsaddiq means "righteous/in the right," not simply "innocent." EMET's rendering is more precise. (2) PNEUMA uses gender-neutral "people"; EMET/LOGOS use "men" (anashim). (3) PNEUMA adds "hear the case" — not in the Hebrew. **Resolution:** "acquitting the righteous and condemning the wicked" — preserving the tsaddiq/rasha distinction.

---

## V.2 — The beating

- **EMET:** "the judge shall cause him to fall down and to be beaten before his face, according to his wickedness, by number"
- **LOGOS:** "the judge shall cause him to lie down and be beaten in his presence with a number of stripes in proportion to his offense"
- **PNEUMA:** "the judge shall have him lie down and be flogged in his presence with a number of lashes proportionate to his offense"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "fall down" (hippilu); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "lie down." The Hebrew can mean either; LOGOS/PNEUMA's rendering is smoother. (2) EMET: "according to his wickedness, by number"; LOGOS/PNEUMA: "in proportion to his offense." LOGOS/PNEUMA is more idiomatic. (3) PNEUMA: "flogged" vs. EMET/LOGOS: "beaten." **Resolution:** "beaten in his presence with a number of stripes in proportion to his offense."

---

## V.3 — Forty stripes

- **EMET:** "Forty [stripes] he may beat him — he shall not add; lest he add to beat him beyond these [with] many blows, and your brother be dishonored in your eyes."
- **LOGOS:** "Forty stripes may be given him, but not more, lest, if more stripes were added than these, your brother be degraded in your sight."
- **PNEUMA:** "Forty lashes may be given, but no more. If more blows than that are administered, your fellow Israelite will be publicly degraded in your eyes."

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "dishonored" (qalah); LOGOS: "degraded"; PNEUMA: "publicly degraded." (2) PNEUMA: "your fellow Israelite" for "your brother" (achikha) — interpretive expansion. (3) PNEUMA adds "publicly" — not in the Hebrew. **Resolution:** "dishonored" — qalah means to make light of; the public dimension is implied, not added.

---

## V.4 — Ox muzzling

- **EMET:** "You shall not muzzle an ox while it is threshing."
- **LOGOS:** "You shall not muzzle an ox when it is treading out the grain."
- **PNEUMA:** "Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain."

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "threshing" (disho); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "treading out the grain." LOGOS/PNEUMA is more explicit. (2) PNEUMA uses imperative "Do not" vs. EMET/LOGOS: "You shall not." Minor stylistic variation. **Resolution:** "You shall not muzzle an ox while it is threshing" — the shorter, more aphoristic form mirrors the Hebrew.

---

## V.5 — Levirate marriage opening

- **EMET:** "When brothers dwell together and one of them dies and he has no son, the wife of the dead one shall not be outside to a stranger-man; her brother-in-law shall come in to her and take her to himself as a wife and perform the duty of a brother-in-law [yibbum] toward her."
- **LOGOS:** "If brothers dwell together, and one of them dies and has no son, the wife of the dead man shall not be married outside the family to a stranger. Her husband's brother shall go in to her and take her as his wife and perform the duty of a husband's brother to her."
- **PNEUMA:** "When brothers are living together and one of them dies without leaving a son, his widow must not marry outside the family. Her husband's brother shall take her as his wife and fulfill the duty of a brother-in-law to her."

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "the wife of the dead one" vs. LOGOS: "the wife of the dead man" vs. PNEUMA: "his widow." (2) EMET: "outside to a stranger-man" vs. LOGOS: "outside the family to a stranger" vs. PNEUMA: "outside the family." (3) LOGOS: "husband's brother" vs. EMET/PNEUMA: "brother-in-law." (4) PNEUMA omits EMET's [yibbum] gloss. (5) LOGOS: "go in to her" vs. PNEUMA: "take her as his wife" — PNEUMA omits the consummation language. **Resolution:** Consensus retains "brother-in-law" and [yibbum] gloss; "the wife of the dead man" is clearest.

---

## V.7 — Sister-in-law's speech

- **EMET:** "My brother-in-law refuses to raise up for his brother a name in Yisra'el — he has not been willing to perform the duty of a brother-in-law for me."
- **LOGOS:** "My husband's brother refuses to perpetuate his brother's name in Israel; he will not perform the duty of a husband's brother to me."
- **PNEUMA:** "My brother-in-law refuses to carry on his brother's name in Israel. He will not do his duty as my brother-in-law."

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "raise up a name" vs. LOGOS: "perpetuate his brother's name" vs. PNEUMA: "carry on his brother's name." The Hebrew is lehaqim shem — "to raise up a name." (2) LOGOS: "husband's brother" consistently; EMET/PNEUMA: "brother-in-law." **Resolution:** "raise up for his brother a name" — preserves the Hebrew idiom.

---

## V.9 — The chalitzah declaration

- **EMET:** "So shall it be done to the man who does not build up the house of his brother."
- **LOGOS:** "So shall it be done to the man who does not build up his brother's house."
- **PNEUMA:** "This is what is done to the man who will not keep his brother's family line alive."

**Differences:** PNEUMA paraphrases "keep his brother's family line alive" vs. EMET/LOGOS: "build up the house of his brother." The Hebrew is lo yivneh et beit achiv — "does not build the house of his brother." PNEUMA's rendering is interpretive. **Resolution:** "does not build up his brother's house" — follows the Hebrew idiom.

---

## V.10 — Name of the house

- **EMET:** "the house of the one whose sandal was pulled off"
- **LOGOS:** "The house of the unsandaled."
- **PNEUMA:** "the House of the Sandal-Removed."

**Differences:** Three different renderings of beit chaluts ha-na'al. LOGOS is the most concise; EMET most literal; PNEUMA most dramatic. **Resolution:** "the house of the one whose sandal was removed" — balances literalness with readability.

---

## V.11 — Fight scenario

- **EMET:** "When men fight together, a man and his brother"
- **LOGOS:** "When men fight with one another"
- **PNEUMA:** "If two men are fighting"

**Differences:** EMET preserves "a man and his brother" (ish ve-achiv) — the same phrase as in the legal context of v.5, emphasizing the community dimension. LOGOS/PNEUMA omit "his brother." **Resolution:** Retain "a man and his brother" — the phrase is legally significant.

---

## V.13 — Differing weights

- **EMET:** "You shall not have in your bag differing stones [for weights] — a large and a small."
- **LOGOS:** "You shall not have in your bag two kinds of weights, a large and a small."
- **PNEUMA:** "Do not carry two different weights in your bag — one heavy and one light."

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "differing stones" (aven va-aven) — the Hebrew idiom "a stone and a stone"; LOGOS: "two kinds of weights"; PNEUMA: "two different weights." (2) PNEUMA: "one heavy and one light" — unpacks the idiom. (3) Imperative vs. declarative form. **Resolution:** "two kinds of weights" (LOGOS) — clear and accurate, capturing the "a stone and a stone" idiom functionally.

---

## V.14 — Differing measures

- **EMET:** "differing measures [ephah]"
- **LOGOS:** "two kinds of measures"
- **PNEUMA:** "two different measuring containers"

**Differences:** EMET retains "[ephah]" gloss. PNEUMA: "measuring containers" is more specific than the Hebrew eyfah (a dry measure). **Resolution:** "two kinds of measures" — parallel with v.13.

---

## V.15 — Honest weights and days

- **EMET:** "A whole and just stone [weight] you shall have; a whole and just ephah you shall have"
- **LOGOS:** "A full and just weight you shall have, a full and just measure you shall have"
- **PNEUMA:** "You must have accurate and honest weights and measures"

**Differences:** (1) EMET: "whole and just" (shlemah ve-tsedek); LOGOS: "full and just"; PNEUMA: "accurate and honest" — paraphrases both terms. (2) PNEUMA merges both sentences into one. The Hebrew even shlemah ve-tsedek — "a complete and just weight." **Resolution:** "A full and just weight... a full and just measure" — follows LOGOS; shlemah = full/complete is well rendered as "full."

---

## V.16 — Abomination formula

- **EMET:** "For an abomination to the LORD your God is everyone who does these things, everyone who acts unjustly."
- **LOGOS:** "For all who do such things, all who act dishonestly, are an abomination to the LORD your God."
- **PNEUMA:** "For everyone who acts dishonestly in these matters is detestable to the LORD your God."

**Differences:** (1) EMET preserves the Hebrew word order (predicate first: "an abomination to the LORD" is the subject). (2) LOGOS restructures more naturally. (3) PNEUMA: "detestable" for to'evah vs. EMET/LOGOS: "abomination." To'evah is a strong technical cultic term; "abomination" is the traditional rendering. **Resolution:** "For all who do such things, all who act dishonestly, are an abomination to the LORD your God" — LOGOS word order with "abomination" preserved.

---

## V.18 — Description of Amalek's attack

- **EMET:** "struck down all the stragglers at your rear when you were faint and weary"
- **LOGOS:** "cut off your tail, those who were lagging behind you" — noting the Hebrew va-yezanev as "tailed you"
- **PNEUMA:** "attacked all who were lagging behind; they had no fear of God"

**Differences:** (1) LOGOS uniquely translates va-yezanev as "cut off your tail" — an unusual English phrase. EMET has "struck down all the stragglers at your rear." (2) PNEUMA: "they had no fear of God" vs. EMET: "he did not fear God" — PNEUMA shifts from singular to plural (Amalek is treated as collective vs. individual). **Resolution:** "struck down all who were lagging behind at your rear" — singular "he did not fear God" follows EMET/LOGOS (Amalek as corporate singular entity).

---

## V.19 — "Memory" vs. "name"

- **EMET:** "you shall blot out the memory of Amalek"
- **LOGOS:** "you shall blot out the memory of Amalek"
- **PNEUMA:** "you shall blot out the name of Amalek"

**Differences:** PNEUMA: "name" for zekher; EMET/LOGOS: "memory." Zekher encompasses both name and memory; "memory" is the more common English translation. **Resolution:** "memory" — EMET/LOGOS.

---

*Total disagreements logged: 15*
*Arbiter: SOPHIA*
